summary: DHH describes how a growing number of classmates have adopted wireless-equipped iBooks with SubEthaEdit, a collaborative text editor, to collectively take notes and discuss lectures in real time. The collaborative note-taking produces stronger notes and deeper engagement with the material. The practice is spreading virally through the classroom as onlookers see the benefits firsthand. Beyond lectures, the tools reduce the need for in-person meetings by enabling remote collaboration on group assignments via SubEthaEdit and voice-over-iChat.
key_points: SubEthaEdit enables real-time collaborative note-taking across multiple students spread throughout an auditorium, producing higher-quality notes than individual efforts
The adoption is spreading virally — from five users to potentially twelve — driven by visible, impressive demonstrations rather than deliberate evangelism
Remote collaboration tools reduce the need for face-to-face meetings, foreshadowing DHH's later advocacy for remote work
The group plans to broadcast lectures live via iSight and QuickTime Broadcaster, enabling students to attend from home
money_quotes: It allows the five (and counting) of us to collaborate on the note-taking process and to discuss the lecture as it happens in a non-intrusive manner.
The final set of notes is stronger and everyone gets increased intimacy with the material.
As we sit spread across the auditorium, this phenomenon infects anyone sitting next to one of our participants.
A reduced need for face time is the most apparent as almost any discussion or assignment can be handled through the combination of SubEthaEdit and voice-over-iChat.
I certainly wouldn't mind to be able to follow morning lectures from the comfort of a robe in my home.
key_insight: Real-time collaborative software can organically transform how groups work together, spreading virally through visible benefits and reducing the need for physical co-presence — a theme DHH would champion for decades to come.
